coredata.dc:description: As a new kind of carbon material, bio-based carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention, attributed to their excellent properties and widespread applications. In particular, biomass hydrothermal conversion has become a popular technology for bio-based CDs production, due to its advantages in terms of friendliness to the environment, effective cost, and simple operation. This article reviews the formation mechanisms, properties, and applications of bio-based hydrothermal CDs comprehensively. The CDs formation mechanisms were summarized from the perspective of biomass (such as lignocellulosic biomass and algae) and its model compositions (such as carbohydrate, protein, and lignin). The properties of bio-based hydrothermal CDs including microstructure, chemical structure and composition, toxicity and biocompatibility, and optical performance were discussed. Meanwhile, the applications of bio-based CDs in biological imaging, sensor, photoelectric device, catalyst, and disease treatment were outlined as well. Furthermore, the co-production of CDs and value-added biofuels from biomass hydrothermal process is proposed and further related research directions were recommended.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new kind of carbon material, bio-based carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention, attributed to their excellent properties and widespread applications. In particular, biomass hydrothermal conversion has become a popular technology for bio-based CDs production, due to its advantages in terms of friendliness to the environment, effective cost, and simple operation. This article reviews the formation mechanisms, properties, and applications of bio-based hydrothermal CDs comprehensively. The CDs formation mechanisms were summarized from the perspective of biomass (such as lignocellulosic biomass and algae) and its model compositions (such as carbohydrate, protein, and lignin). The properties of bio-based hydrothermal CDs including microstructure, chemical structure and composition, toxicity and biocompatibility, and optical performance were discussed. Meanwhile, the applications of bio-based CDs in biological imaging, sensor, photoelectric device, catalyst, and disease treatment were outlined as well. Furthermore, the co-production of CDs and value-added biofuels from biomass hydrothermal process is proposed and further related research directions were recommended.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) were firstly discovered in the electro-catalytic process of carbon nanotubes accidentally since 2004 [1], they have received increasing attention as new-fashioned carbon materials with huge application potentials. CDs are nanomaterials with carbon skeleton structures that always contain amounts of functional groups on surfaces and frequently display spherical shapes with sizes less than 10 nm. Because of their nanometer size in three dimensions, CDs are defined as zero-dimensional carbon materials and have become robust materials for improving energy utilization efficiency, environmental management, health protection, and precision instrumentation technologies [2].

CDs exhibit several unique characteristics and could be applied in a variety of fields, because of their particular compositions and structures. To begin with, the π-π* transition of the CC bond in CDs carbon nuclei enables them to strongly absorb UV light and generate fluorescence signals (400–710 nm), making CDs show a potential to be used in fluorescence imaging [3] and substance detection [4]. Secondly, CDs always dissolve in water readily and are easy to be functionalized due to their nanometer size and abundant surface functional groups [5]. Thirdly, CDs are mostly made of carbon (C), hydrogen (H), and oxygen (O) elements with molecular weights ranging from several thousand to dozens of thousands. As a result, CDs show good biocompatibility [6] and are expected to substitute bio-toxic semiconductor quantum dots or traditional fluorescent dyes [7,8]. Fourthly, CDs display oxidation-reduction ability, electron transfer performance, and electron storage capacity as their high light-absorption cross sections and straightforward tunability through diameter variation and surface ligand structures [9], so that they display enormous potential in the fields of photoelectric conversion and energy storage [8,10].

The commonly used carbon sources of CDs mainly include carbon nanotube, graphene, coal, citric acid, carbohydrate, and other traditional chemicals at the beginning [32], which have problems such as expensive raw materials, complex preparation process, difficult to purify products, and residual toxic chemical reagents. Therefore, it is important to identify the green and cheap sources for CDs preparation. Renewable natural biomass materials have gradually been used to produce bio-based CDs because they are widely available, cheap, and environmentally friendly [23]. Up till now, Gelidium amansii [33], Enteromorpha prolifera [34], sweet potato peel [35], Lactobacillus plantarum [35], soybean [36], Magnolia flower [37], broccoli leaves [38], Eichhornia crassipes [39], Sargassum fluitans [7], Spirulina species [40], lutein [41], and some other biomass have been hydrothermally treated to prepare CDs successfully.

The species diversity of biomass and the changing hydrothermal conditions make the yields (0.75–95%) [34,42–44] and properties of bio-based CDs vary without obvious regularity. In addition, the formation mechanism of these CDs is still unclear since the complex chemical compositions of biomass and a great challenge to in-situ characterize the intermediates from the hydrothermal process [15]. Therefore, to promote the development of biomass hydrothermal CDs technology, it is important to explore the key pathways to generate CDs from the point of chemical components, reveal relationships between biomass components and CDs structures, and confirm suitable properties of CDs for application.

The bio-based hydrothermal CDs usually exist in the aqueous product from biomass hydrothermal process, meanwhile, the process produces gas, organic liquid, and solid products. Such products have been paid attention to for obtaining high-valued biofuels, such as biogas, bio-oil, and biochar [45–48]. Therefore, the co-production of CDs and biofuels from biomass hydrothermal conversion could provide new guidance for enhancing the overall environmental and economic value to use biomass.

To our knowledge, a comprehensive review focused on formation mechanisms, properties, and applications of bio-based hydrothermal CDs is still lacking. Additionally, the co-production of CDs and biofuels via biomass hydrothermal conversion has not been discussed before. This work reviews the co-production of bio-based CDs and biofuels, of which the aims are to:
•
Reveal the formation mechanism of bio-based CDs during the hydrothermal process through the view of biomass (such as lignocellulosic biomass and algae) and their model compositions (such as carbohydrate, lignin, and protein).
•
Summarize properties (such as microstructure, chemical structure and composition, toxicity and biocompatibility, and optical performance) and applications (such as biological imaging, sensor, photoelectric device, catalyst, and disease treatment) of bio-based CDs.
•
Discuss the possibility of co-production of CDs and biofuels (biogas, bio-oil, and biochar) via biomass hydrothermal conversion and recommend further research directions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-based CDs formation mechanism via hydrothermal process
ce:section.ce:para: Both the model compositions of biomass (such as carbohydrate, lignin, and protein) and biomass (such as lignocellulose, algae, and municipal waste) have been used for CDs production in recent years [20,39,49–51]. In the hydrothermal process, these components undergo different transformation pathways, resulting in the formation of CDs with various yields, structures, and properties. The generation mechanism of CDs during the hydrothermal process is discussed from the view of biomass model compositions and biomass themself in the following section.

Carbohydrate, represented by a general formula Cx(H2O)y, is the main component of biomass, such as lignocellulose and algae. Up till now, many kinds of carbohydrate model compounds, such as glucose, sucrose, starch, hemicellulose, cellulose, and pectin have been used to prepare carbohydrate-based CDs successfully [52]. It is considered that oligosaccharides and polysaccharides will be hydrolyzed into single sugar units (for instance, starch hydrolyzed to be glucose), and subsequently converted into carbohydrate-based CDs through a complicated dehydration process (see Fig. 2a) [36,52]. In the first step, different acids or bases are selected and added to facilitate the hydrolysis of carbohydrates into single sugars in some studies [53], by providing catalytic points to break macromolecular chains [35]. It is widely accepted that the single sugar unit (such as glucose) is transformed into 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF), and then the HMF is further dehydrated into aromatic carbonaceous structures to form CDs carbon skeleton [20,54].

Papaioannou et al. [55] suggested that glucose was isomerized to fructose followed by eliminating three water molecules to form HMF, which was dehydrated and polymerized into small polymeric nanoclusters and started growing into carbonaceous spheres subsequently. The condensation and cycloaddition reactions of HMF produce soluble polymers and bring aromatic clusters. These clusters will increase to the supersaturation point at a critical concentration by aromatization and carbonization, and result in a nucleation burst [35]. Then the nuclei grow isotropically and uniformly during the solute diffused towards particles surfaces, and the grown carbon substances are oxidized to give the obtained CDs improved properties [54], such as excellent solubility in the aqueous solution [38]. Zhao et al. [56] conjectured that hemicellulose decomposed into furfural and furfuryl alcohol; meanwhile, condensation, chain breaking, and chain forming reactions occurred to form cyclic compounds. These compounds underwent polymerization and condensation to generate aromatics with oxygen-containing functional groups, and the aromatics could be considered precursors of CDs.

Lignin, with amounts varying over approximately 10–45% of lignocellulosic biomass [57], is an important component to ensure a stable structure of lignocellulosic biomass. The lignin polymer is rich in aromatic ring structures and formed by the connections of hydroxyphenyl, syringyl, and guaiacyl units. Xu et al. [58] had proved that the aromatic rings of syringyl and guaiacyl units were identified in the two-dimensional heteronuclear single-quantum coherence spectroscopy nuclear magnetic resonance spectra of alkali lignin-based CDs, which demonstrated that some interunit linkages and aromatic rings of lignin were present in its hydrothermal CDs.

Under hydrothermal conditions, some weak phenylpropane units in lignin degrade into small molecules via hydrolysis and dissolve in the aqueous phase [8] (see Fig. 2b). With increasing the hydrothermal temperature, water molecules are dissociated into hydrogen ions and hydroxide ions. The hydroxide ion could attack the primary alcohol structure of soluble molecules to remove its hydrogen atom, and a carbonyl group is formed [59]. Subsequently, O–OH– bonds can be formed when hydrogen atoms are closed to oxygen atoms [59]. Consequently, the carbonyl group contained phenylpropane units will self-assemble by the above hydrogen bonding interaction to form nanoclusters lignin-based CDs.

Acids, such as phosphoric acid [49], nitric acid [60], sulfuric acid [61], o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid [62], and citric acid [58] are applied during the hydrothermal process when preparing lignin-based CDs. Liu et al. [49] found that acid-assisted hydrothermal treatment catalyzed the scission or cleavages of ether bonds, thus promoting the lignin degradative transformation of carbon materials with well-established structures because the effective cleavage of ether bonds was critical to synthesize high-quality materials. Ding et al. [42] considered that most bonds cleavages had occurred intensively when hydrothermally treated alkali lignin at 180 °C for 0.5 h. In addition, the use of acids can dope CDs with P, N, or S containing groups, which might provide effective active sites on CDs and make CDs display excellent optical, catalytic, or electrochemical properties [49,62].

Most of the nitrogen in biomass is in the form of protein, which is the basic component that makes up cells and participates in their growth and metabolism. During the hydrothermal process, proteins are hydrolyzed into amino acids, then amines and low-molecular-weight organic acids are produced by decarboxylation and deamination reactions of amino acids (see Fig. 2c). In more detail, decarboxylation of amino acids will produce carbonic acid and amines, and deamination of amino acids will generate ammonia and organic acids under hydrothermal conditions [63]. As to these two paths, Liu et al. [64] considered that decarboxylation was more likely to occur than deamination in the protein hydrothermal process, while Sato et al. [63] held the viewpoint that the occurrence ratio of deamination to decarboxylation differed depending on amino acids types. Subsequently, condensation and cross-linking of amines and organic acids, which could be seen as CDs precursors, are followed to generate CDs [64]. However, a more detailed reaction path for condensation and cross-linking reactions has not yet been reported.

Lignocellulose is one of the most common types of biomass, its cellulose and hemicellulose are decomposed and remain as dispersion supernatants under hydrothermal treatment with temperatures above 150 °C [8]. They are transformed into oligomeric-reducing sugars and furfural derivatives through dehydration and chain breaking reactions [64], whereas lignin barely decomposes at a temperature lower than 170 °C and degrades into aromatic acids/ethers and phenols under a high temperature hydrothermal process [56] (see Fig. 3 ). Meanwhile, these hydrolytic products undergo chain breaking, condensation, and chain forming reactions to produce a series of heterocyclic compounds. Subsequently, numerous oxygen-containing condensates are formed by further polymerization and aromatic condensation, which could be considered precursors of CDs. Lastly, these condensates are connected and contracted to each other to form CDs [56]. Park et al. [8] proposed that the decomposition of cellulose and hemicellulose was very rapid and lignin was separated from lignocellulosic biomass; subsequently, the separated lignin molecules approached each other and led to condensation and carbonization reactions. Lots of works have prepared lignocellulose-based CDs by incorporating acids into the hydrothermal process as well [8,65,66]. Liu et al. [35] regarded that hydrolysis, dehydration, and decomposition of lignocellulose compounds produced soluble ketones, furfural, and organic acids with the addition of acid. The produced organic acids can be used as acid resources for running acid-catalyzed reactions to produce more soluble matters as CDs precursors.

As to algae-based CDs, their formation pathways relevant study is quite scarce up till now. Carbohydrates and proteins, as typical components of algae, exert important influences on the formation and functionality of CDs [64]. Unlike carbohydrate or protein monomers, interactions among these major components appeared to be important to form CDs during the algae hydrothermal transformation, which is primarily attributed to the Maillard reaction among the hydrolysis products of carbohydrates and proteins (i.e., oligomeric-reducing sugars and amino acids) [51]. The Maillard reaction generates a variety of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds through condensation, Amadori rearrangement, and Strecker degradation reactions [67]. Subsequently, these compounds undergo cross-linking, polymerization, and carbonization reactions to form nitrogen-doped CDs [64]. However, Sankaranarayanan et al. [51] suggested that nitrogen-containing polymers produced from Maillard reactions were not involved in CDs formation owing to no nitrogen contained in the Macrocystis pyrifera pyrolyzed bio-oil-based CDs. They conjectured that the reason for this result was the inadequate hydrothermal temperature as low as 170 °C. Furthermore, another important component in algae, neither the lipid itself as a carbon precursor to prepare CDs nor its influence on CDs generation has been reported till now. Overall, the suggested algae-based CDs formation mechanism is still unclear and needs to be further explored.

As shown in Table 2 , particle sizes of carbohydrate-based CDs, lignin-based CDs, and protein-based CDs are around 2.7–19.1 nm, 2.0–10.0 nm, and 2.0–59.0 nm when prepared by various biomass model compounds under different hydrothermal conditions. The lignin-based CDs appeared to have the smallest particle size, while protein-based CDs displayed a larger size distribution when prepared by the hydrothermal method. It is speculated that the aromatic structure in lignin contributes to the formation of the carbon core of CDs, or that such a structure can become a carbon core simply, while the nitrogen element in protein may affect the formation and uniformity of the carbon core of CDs when protein is used as CDs carbon precursor. From another point, Briscoe et al. [44] supported that the size of CDs depends on the precursor size that a smaller molecule precursor generated smaller CDs.

The size of carbohydrate-based CDs particles increases with increasing hydrothermal temperature and time [20,50,68,69], and appears to show a smaller size with the addition of auxiliary chemical solvents [43,44]. For instance, the particle size of hemicellulose-based CDs prepared by alkaline soluble xylan with the addition of NH4OH solution appeared about one-third the size of bare CDs [43]. The size of modified cellulose-based CDs prepared by chemically purified cellulose and ethylenediamine was 6–8 nm, which was smaller than 10 nm of Cladophora rupestris extracted cellulose-based CDs, even though the latter CDs were prepared at a higher hydrothermal temperature [44,72].

On the contrary, several studies have confirmed that additional chemical agents can make lignin-based CDs a little bigger. For example, empty fruit bunches extracted lignin-based CDs displayed 2.9–4.1 nm size with the addition of urea during the hydrothermal process, which was slightly larger than 2–3 nm of the CDs without the addition of urea [59]. Adding phosphoric acid [49], nitric acid [60], and sulfuric acid [61] demonstrated similar results as well.

In terms of protein-based CDs, the addition of 1.6% sodium pyrophosphate or improvement of hydrothermal temperature to 180 °C from 160 °C could reduce the particle size of glucosamine hydrochloride-based CDs by half [77,78]. The ethanol auxiliary prepared bovine serum albumin-based CDs displayed a little bigger but more uniform particle sizes than those assisted by the auxiliary of 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine [80,81].

As to biomass with complex compositions, lignocellulose-based and algae-based CDs show particles sizes about 1.0–13.0 nm and 0.7–50.0 nm, respectively (see Table 3 ). The algae-based CDs appear to display a wider particle size range than lignocellulose-based CDs, which might be due to algae containing some protein while lignocellulose almost contains no protein currently. Because of the variety of species and compositions of lignocellulose, the microstructure of lignocellulose-based CDs appears to have miscellaneous variation trends, even though relevant research is widely studied.

Liu et al. [85] found that the particle sizes of ethanol extracted bamboo leaves-based CDs almost had not changed with the addition of sodium carbonate during the hydrothermal process. Differently, the extracted waste tea-based CDs became one-ten smaller in size when prepared CDs with the addition of cupric acetate monohydrate [87,88]. Regarding empty fruit bunch-based CDs, the CDs' size changed little when phytic acid was used as an additive, increased to about two times when used melamine during preparation, and enhanced to about four times with the addition of thionyl chloride during the hydrothermal process [8]. In terms of algae-based CDs, Li et al. [67] used glucose and lysine to simulate the Maillard reaction during the hydrothermal process and found that the size of nanoparticles exhibited a decreasing trend with the extension of hydrothermal time.

To confirm the chemical structures and compositions of CDs, the obtained CDs are usually analyzed by the Raman spectrum, the Fourier Transform Infrared spectrum (FT-IR), and the X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

The Raman spectra of CDs frequently show broad peaks at 1233–1370 cm−1 and 1408–1600 cm−1 [36,51,55,60,68,91,94], which exhibits that D-band (a common feature for sp3 carbon defects) and G-band (plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbon) carbon structures appear in CDs [20,84]. Generally, peak intensities of the D-band and G-band reflect the disordered carbon structure and ordered graphite structure contents in bio-based CDs, where a higher intensity ratio of the G-band to D-band indicates better crystalline and higher graphitization degree [20]. Shen et al. [20] found that the glucose-based CDs had a better crystalline structure than citric acid-based CDs when prepared CDs with the same procedure. Because the former CDs showed a higher intensity ratio of G-band to D-band than the latter CDs in Raman spectra. Additionally, the D-band of glucose-based CDs became stronger when UV irradiated glucose for a longer time till to 48 h, and the ratio of D-band to G-band increased to be 0.7 at 48 h of irradiated time from 0.1 at 24 h of irradiated time [70].

Many previous results from FT-IR and XPS spectra have confirmed that the obtained CDs normally conclude OH (3300–3650 cm−1, O1s 532.5 eV), CH (2930–2870 cm−1; C1s 284.9 eV), CO (1770–1646 cm−1, C1s 287.3–288.7 eV, O1s 531.0–532.1 eV), CC (1661–1468 cm−1, C1s 284.5–284.8 eV), C–O–C (1201–1058 cm−1, C1s 286.2–287.3 eV, O1s 532.3–532.8 eV), CC (1043 cm−1, C1s 284.5–286.0 eV), –COO (1400 cm−1, O1s 532.0–533.3 eV) groups and bonds [54,56,65,87,95]. Furthermore, CS (624–617 cm−1, C1s 285.7–286.0 eV, S2p 163.60–169.30 eV), NH (3289–3063 cm−1, 1381–1337 cm−1, N1s 401.3–401.5 eV), CN (1180–1210 cm−1; C1s 285.4–285.7 eV, N1s 399.5–400.5 eV), CN (1652 cm−1, C1s 287.9–288.7 eV, N1s 398.7–399.0 eV), –CONH– (1593 cm−1), PC (P2p 131.6–132.00 eV), PO (1170–1038 cm−1; P2p 135.5 eV), PO (1265 cm−1; O1s 531.0 eV, P2p 133.70 eV) bonds also have been detected in E. crassipes-based CDs [39], pork rib bones-based CDs [29], L. plantarum-based CDs [101], S. fluitans-based CDs [7], NH4OH modified xylan-based CDs [43], ammonia modified citric acid-based CDs [102], sulfamic acid modified pomelo juice-based CDs [66] and phytic acid modified empty fruit bunch-based CDs [8] as well, which means that these bio-based CDs are doped with nitrogen, sulfur or phosphorus element, and verifies that bio-based CDs contain multiple functional groups.

In addition to confirming functional groups, XPS results could give insight into revealing the elemental compositions of CDs [35] (Table 4 ). The carbon and oxygen contents in bio-based CDs are 37.8–82.6% and 5.2–42.9%, respectively. Apart from that, 0–16.9% nitrogen, 0–3.6% sulfur, 2.2% phosphorus, 3.95% Cu or 2.23% Mg are detected in bio-based CDs as well (see Table 4). Li et al. [67] found that the carbon and nitrogen contents of glucose-based and lysine-based CDs decreased while hydrogen and oxygen contents increased with the extension of reaction time, which was possibly due to the hydration reaction occurring among unsaturated bonds during the hydrothermal process. The C/O ratio of CDs was greatly increased to 3.27 from 1.42 of its carbon precursor original xylan, which indicated that high dehydration of xylan occurred in the process of hydrothermal treatment [43]. Similar results were detected in alkali lignin-based CDs [42] and nitric acid modified lignin-based CDs [60]. Several studies have confirmed that carbon was the dominant element in CDs, which made CDs low- or non-toxicity [103]. CDs have many oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, making CDs water-soluble and easy to be modified [104]. The nitrogen element doped on CDs is proved to benefit the photoluminescence property of CDs [105], the sulfur atoms contained in CDs could act as electron-donors and improve the photocurrent of CDs [106], the phosphorus element in CDs shows an ability to increase the wavelength of emitted light [8], and the metal ion such as Mg appears to enhance the PLQY of CDs [75].

Toxicity and biocompatibility are essential properties for bio-based CDs' potential application in the fields such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and disease diagnosis [43]. CDs prepared from different biomass raw materials might exhibit different toxicity and biocompatibility, even prepared under the same operation parameters of the hydrothermal process. For instance, HeLa cells showed good viability in glucose-based CDs solutions with a concentration of 0–1000 μg/mL CDs and showed biocompatibility at ascorbic acid-based CDs concentrations lower than 250 μg/mL. However, fructose-based CDs exhibited high toxicity to HeLa cells even with a concentration as low as 1.95 μg/mL [69], which might relate to the oxidative stress reaction of CDs [107].

A lot of studies have been conducted to confirm the toxicity and biocompatibility of bio-based CDs [43,90,93,101]. Lin et al. [101] found that L. plantarum-based CDs showed almost no toxicity and displayed excellent biocompatibility because the CDs did not affect the viability and growth of Escherichia coli cells and animal lung AT II cells. In another study, NH4OH passivated alkaline soluble xylan-based CDs exhibited scarce toxicity with over 80% ATDC5 cells viability even after 48 h cultivation at CDs concentration up to 100 μg/mL [43]. Furthermore, the ethanol passivated Cladophora vagabunda-based CDs showed inactive cytotoxicity on brine shrimp and displayed antibacterial activity against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative E. coli [93]. Additionally, Qi et al. [90] found that the cytotoxicity of nitrogen-doped bio-based CDs was lower than that of bio-based CDs when used rice residue as a carbon precursor and glycine as a nitrogen resource. The HepG2 cell viability remained greater than 85% at 100 mg/mL of rice residue and glycine-based CDs, indicating excellent biocompatibility. The nitrogen doping could reduce CD's toxicity and similar results were confirmed in NH4OH modified alkaline soluble xylan-based CDs as well [43]. The above findings suggested that most of the prepared bio-based CDs had the potential to be used in biology-related fields, and nitrogen-doping modification would be advantageous for the biocompatibility of bio-based CDs for future application.

CDs appear to emit fluorescence under special excitative conditions, such as UV irradiation [2]. To date the exact mechanism may not be fully understood, however, can be mainly attributed to the following: (1) the combination of quantum confinement effect and conjugated π fields caused by CDs cores [108], (2) the influence of carbon skeleton and linked chemical groups on the surface state of CDs [109], (3) the luminescent molecules attached to the surface or present in the interior of CDs [110], and (4) the emission effect caused by cross-linking or atoms doping [111]. As shown in Tables 2 and 3, most bio-based CDs exhibit blue emission, and several bio-based CDs could emit green fluorescence [44,78,112], cyan fluorescence [39,59,64,72,83], and red fluorescence [85,93] under UV illumination. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of bio-based CDs, absorption peaks appeared at 228–270 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of CC and 278–345 nm related to the n-π* of CO bond [29,36,43,70]. For instance, the glucose-based CDs displayed characteristic peaks at 243 nm and 345 nm [70], the soybean-based CDs contained two characteristic peaks at 228 nm and 278 nm [36], the Pectinodesmus sp.-based CDs existed peaks around 236–254 nm and 300 nm [82], and the NH4OH modified alkaline soluble xylan-based CDs showed a weak peak at 273 nm and a shoulder peak around 310 nm [43] in UV–vis spectra, respectively.

In terms of emission characteristics, most bio-based CDs exhibited an excitation dependent fluorescence behavior [36,70], which might be caused by different particle sizes or surface states in the amorphous carbon shell of bio-based CDs near the Fermi level [29,101]. In most cases, the absorption wavelength of bio-based CDs is longer than the corresponding emission wavelength, implying that the absorption energy is higher than the emission energy [2]. The maximum emission wavelengths of orange peel-based CDs [37], Pectinodesmus sp.-based CDs [82], and ethanol modified shrimp-based CDs [95] were at 445 nm, 548 nm, and 475 nm with excitation wavelengths of 360 nm, 480 nm, and 430 nm, respectively. On the contrary, a small number of bio-based CDs also showed up-conversion fluorescence property. The up-conversion fluorescence property of CDs might be attributed to the multi-photon activation process in which simultaneous absorption of two or more photos leads to an emission light with a wavelength shorter than that of excitation light [113]. For instance, the ethanol modified peach blossoms-based CDs emitted in the range of 400 nm to 600 nm when excited by long-wavelength light from 650 nm to 900 nm [89]. The above bio-based CDs could improve light energy conversion efficiency by converting near-infrared light into visible light.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of bio-based CDs, which is defined as the ratio of emitting fluorescence photons number to excitation light photons absorbing number per unit time, is varying between 0.3% and 54.0% when the CDs were prepared by different biomass (Tables 2 and 3). In general, the QY of CDs prepared by lignin or its derivative is higher, followed by protein or its derivative-based CDs, and carbohydrate or its derivative-based CDs (Table 2). The higher QY of lignin-based CDs may relate to the aromatic ring structures of lignin itself [58], while the nitrogen element contained in proteins could passivate CDs directly and make them exhibit higher QY [83].

As to the hydrothermal condition influence on the optical property of bio-based CDs, it is assumed that a higher hydrothermal temperature might assist in the carbonization and surface functionalization of bio-based CDs, thereby a higher reaction temperature will conduce to improving the fluorescence property of bio-based CDs [29]. On the other hand, with the extension of hydrothermal time, the fluorescence intensity of animal waste-based CDs increased significantly from 6 h to 10 h of reaction time and then stayed stable with a longer reaction time [29]. Additionally, pretreatment or modification of feedstock has been proven to affect the optical property of bio-based CDs in many studies [43,70]. It is found that 365 nm UV irradiation could make the photoluminescence of glucose-based CDs show excitation from blue to red luminescence at 24–72 h of irradiation time, owing to the different localization of electron-hole pairs in the isolated sp2 cluster with sp3 matrix [70]. The QY of NH4OH modified alkaline soluble xylan-based CDs reached 16.2% and was much higher than the 2.1% of unmodified CDs, which indicated nitrogen-doping was an effective way to enhance the fluorescence of bio-based CDs [43].

A large number of studies have focused on the applications of bio-based CDs, which mainly include biological imaging [43], sensor [37], photoelectric device [114], catalyst [59], and disease treatment [115] (see Fig. 4 ).

As mentioned above, the bio-based CDs show particle sizes about 1.0–59.0 nm, display good water solubility, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, as well as particular fluorescence emission characteristics, which make the CDs exhibit special advantages in the field of biological imaging (see Fig. 4a).

In the past few years, most bioimaging experiments have been carried out by using various animal cells, microalgae, and bacteria. For example, murine embryonic stem cell [43], breast cancer cell MCF-7 [116], Escherichia coli cell [96], Saccharomyces cerevisiae cell [96], embryonic kidney HEK 293 cell [96], HepG2 cell [66], Thalassiosira weisslflogii cell [92], and Dunaliella salina cell [92] were selected to evaluate the bioimaging capability of NH4OH modified alkaline soluble xylan-based CDs [43], cow manure-based CDs [116], E. coli genomic DNA-based CDs [96], sulfamic acid modified pomelo juice-based CDs [66], and D. salina-based CDs [92]. For instance, the HepG2 cells loaded with sulfamic acid modified pomelo juice-based CDs showed blue, green, and red colors under excitations at 405 nm, 488 nm, and 560 nm respectively, showing that the CDs are promising in bioimaging [66].

In contrast, bioimaging on higher plant cells has rarely been reported, because plant cells always exhibit complex cell structures with cell walls, fibrous tissues, vacuoles, and chloroplasts. The conventional probes are difficult to pass through the heavily thickened wall of plant guard cells, thereby leading to the difficulty in detecting fluorescence signals. Zhang et al. [117] found that the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped Nannochloropsis biocrude oil-based CDs can pass through the thickened wall of Arabidopsis thaliana guard cells, root hairs, and lateral roots and emit blue fluorescence signal when excited at 405 nm, because of the high solubility and excellent biocompatibility of the CDs. However, the CDs are difficult to enter some vacuoles, and dark areas appear during the test, since the high osmotic pressure inside vacuoles.

Bio-based CDs have received much attention in the field of sensors (see Fig. 4b) due to their extremely small size, large surface area, high activity, and specific fluorescence properties [15]. Many bio-based CDs display particular responses to appointed substances and have been developed as fluorescent sensors for metal ions, anions, and molecules [2,15,118]. The sensor detection principles of CDs are divided into fluorescence quenching (turn-off) [88], fluorescence enhancement (turn-on) [119], fluorescence recovery (off-on) [119], and fluorescence secondary quenching (off-on-off) [87]. The turn-off or turn-on sensor refers to the quenching or enhancement of CDs' fluorescence by the detection target directly [119]. The fluorescence of the off-on sensor could be restored when adding the subject matter to a bio-based CDs system containing fluorescence quenching agents [120]. The off-on-off bio-based CDs sensor is constructed by fluorescence off by a quenching agent, fluorescence on caused by a fluorescence recovery agent, and fluorescence disappears again due to the target agent [87].

Fe3+ is the most widely detected metal ion in bio-based CDs sensor applications [37,40,54,56,62,87,90,92] since the functional groups such as carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups present on bio-based CDs can be effectively combined with iron ions, resulting in fluorescence quenching [2]. For instance, the orange peel-based CDs, Paulownia leaves-based CDs, Ginkgo biloba leaves-based CDs, and Magnolia flower-based CDs have sensitive responses to Fe3+ ions in the concentration range of 0.2–100 μM and the limit of detection could be as low as 0.073 μM with 94–108% of recoveries [37]. Additionally, detections of Pb2+ [85], Hg2+ [94], Cr6+ [86], Al3+ [121], Cu2+ [122], and Co2+ [123] have also become popular recently. Liu et al. [85] prepared ethanol extracted bamboo leaves-based CDs and sodium carbonate modified ethanol extracted bamboo leaves-based CDs as fluorescent sensors with dual-emission and three-emission properties for sensitive detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in river water. The detection limits could achieve at 0.14 nM and 0.22 nM for Pb2+ and Hg2+, respectively.

In terms of anions, PO4 3− [124], O2 •− (superoxide anion) [125], ClO− [126], I− [127] and S2− [128] have been detected by bio-based CDs successfully. The ethanol soluble hongcaitai-based CDs were proven to be successful in determining ClO− in tap water with an efficiently quenched fluorescence at acidic conditions and a detection limit low to 0.015 μM [126]. Sensitive detection of O2 •− is of great significance and challenge, since it is one of the most important reactive oxygen species in environmental and hazardous materials. Yue et al. [125] found that the fluorescence of prepared CDs could be selectively recovered upon the addition of O2 •− and showed good linearity with O2 •− concentration, and the detection limit of O2 •− was estimated down to 25 pM in aqueous solution.

In addition, bio-based CDs can be used to detect some molecules, such as nonylphenol [129], H2O2 [40], ascorbic acid [130], L-cysteine [87], tetracycline [90], dimethoate [29], Sudan I [61], hyaluronidase [78], coenzyme A [84], and nucleic acid [7]. For instance, the fluorescence intensity ratio of rice residue/glycine-based CDs solution and this solution with the addition of tetracycline increased with the enhancement of tetracycline concentration and displayed a perfect linear relationship among 3.32–32.26 μM tetracycline concentrations with the detection limit low to 0.24 μM [90]. Hu et al. [84] found that coenzyme A could restore the luminescence of water chestnut and onion-based S/N doped CDs, which could establish an off-on fluorescence probe for coenzyme A determination with a 0.03–40 μM linear range and a 0.01 μM detection limit of coenzyme A concentration.

Bio-based CDs, as new light absorber members, have been investigated to be used in photoelectric devices (Fig. 4c), because they show excitation dependent fluorescence emission performance [2], electron excitation property, as well as tunable energy levels [36]. Thereby, the bio-based CDs have been applied in solar cells [50,53], light emitting diodes (LEDs) [31], photodetectors [131] and supercapacitors [38,132].

The solar cells made with the addition of CDs have been reported to show outstanding improvements in solar-to-electric conversion efficiency and stability [39,133]. For example, the solar power conversion efficiencies of solar cells with adding of L-arginine-based CDs, L-cysteine-based CDs, or lobster-based CDs as sensitizers were 0.4%, 0.3%, or 0.2%, respectively, which were hugely higher than the 0.004% of solar cells without CDs [50]. Even though their power conversion efficiencies were still too low to be used directly, these solar cells with additions of CDs displayed higher efficiencies than the control group. It might relate to thiol groups and amine groups presented on the bio-based CDs surface, which could bind to the TiO2 surface and led to high light harvest with reduced cluster formation and internal recombination for increasing internal efficiency [50]. Additionally, Meng et al. [36] constructively prepared an innovative bio-based CDs solar cell which could generate electricity both in the daytime and dark. The conversion efficiency of the optimized solar cell was maximized as high as 7.97% with persistent electricity output in the dark for several hours since the photoelectron excitation behaviors of bio-based CDs could contribute to conversing green light from long persistence phosphors in the dark [36].

Bio-based CDs show the potential to replace phosphor for building LEDs, due to their low toxicity, fine stability, and high QY properties. Ge et al. [31] used the ethanolamine modified dehydroabietic acid-based CDs as a color conversion layer to prepare a white LED with 1.36 lm/W of luminous efficacy at 20 mA successfully. The prepared LED could distinguish the true colors of pen caps with CIE chromaticity coordinates (0.3304, 0.3055). The bio-based CDs could be used as photodetectors because of their ability to convert incident light into an electrical signal [133]. The waste paper/urea-based CDs were incorporated with ZnO nanorod arrays to fabricate broadband photodetectors with significantly enhanced photoresponsivity, faster response, and better sensitivity in the visible range. The enhanced sensing of visible light is caused by the excellent ability of nitrogen-doped CDs to absorb visible light and complement of ZnO weakness by CDs functional groups [131]. The utilization of bio-based CDs to prepare supercapacitors has drawn the attention of scientists due to their structural diversities, and tuneable physical and chemical properties [132]. A 2/1 mass ratio of cauliflower leaf wastes-based CDs and graphene oxide were used to prepare electrodes composites, which were sustainable supercapacitor electrodes, exhibited excellent capacitance and superior cycling stability with 278 F/g maximum discharge capacitance at 0.2 A/g and 63% capacitance retention at 1000 mV/s scan rate [38].
Due to their unique photoluminescent behaviors and electron transfer properties, bio-based CDs have been considered active photocatalysts and electrocatalysts (Fig. 4d) [2,134].

It has been verified that many kinds of bio-based CDs could promote the degradation of phenol [20], methyl orange [71], methylene blue [68], malachite green [59], and tetracycline [89], as their effective photocatalytic activities. For instance, a great enhancement in the photocatalytic degradation performance of methyl orange was achieved by incorporating sucrose-based CDs with BiOI as a light absorber [71]. The CDs could enhance the visible light absorption range, serve as electron surface trap sites, and charge separations at the heterojunctions of CDs/BiOI nanosheets. Therefore, about 98.5% of methyl orange was degraded over the CDs/BiOI nanosheets after 50 min of visible light irradiation, which was four times higher than that of BiOI nanosheets [71]. Likewise, the photocatalyst Bi2WO6/Cu2O with the addition of ethanol modified peach blossoms-based CDs exhibited excellent tetracycline degradation capacity under visible light. Which was due to the formation of more electron-hole pairs for effective absorbing entire spectra by the acceleration of CDs' up-conversion effect, the inhibiting of electron-hole pairs by enriched electron transfer with CDs acting as electronic acceptors, and the reduced recombination of photo-generated charge carriers with the formation of heterojunctions [89].

Additionally, CDs were demonstrated as electrocatalysts because their free radical functional groups and high specific surface areas played vital roles in providing CDs with excellent electron transfer efficiency [70,97,135]. Deng et al. [135] used the municipal sludge to prepare a carbon-based material consisting of nitrogen-doped biochar and CDs recently. This material was confirmed to be an efficient catalyst for the electrochemical conversion of CO2 to value-added organic products. The experiment and density functional theory calculations implied that the decoration of CDs on the above catalyst played a pivotal role in the alternative and effective production of ethanol [135]. The soy milk-based nitrogen-doped CDs had been proved to exhibit good electrocatalytic activity towards oxygen reduction reactions, which showed a substantial reduction process under oxygen with the cathodic reduction peak around 0.35 V, whereas no obvious response was detected in the presence of nitrogen [97]. The doped nitrogen in CDs was seen as a key role in enhancing electrocatalytic activity because the atom was found to have a significant influence on the selectivity and catalytic ability of CDs [97].

Bio-based CDs show excellent biocompatibility and high stability, which are small in size and could be cellular uptake rapidly, so they can serve as multifunctional vehicles for drug loading and releasing [2]. Several findings have indicated that bio-based CDs hold great promise for drug delivery and phototherapy techniques with reducing side effects in disease treatment (Fig. 4e).

D'souza et al. [95] prepared nitrogen-doped CDs from dried shrimps and tried to use the CDs to form a traceable drug delivery system for delivering boldine to human breast cancer MCF-7 cells. The associated drug release process could be explained as follows: Firstly, the multifunctional groups on shrimp-based CDs provided them with excellent stability, attractive fluorescence, and high boldine loading efficiency. Secondly, the boldine-loaded CDs went into MCF-7 cells and were mainly localized in the cytoplasm instead of the nucleus. Thirdly, the loaded boldine was released into an acidic microenvironment and entered into the MCF-7 cell nucleus via membrane diffusion [95]. Another study discovered that lychee exocarp-based CDs could load transferrin and photosensitizer onto the surfaces of cancer cells and mouse tumors for image-guided photodynamic therapy [115]. The prepared lychee exocarp-based CDs showed very low biological toxicity and emitted near-infrared fluorescence. During the photodynamic therapy process, the transferrin and photosensitizer loaded CDs were injected into tumor tissues firstly, the photosensitizer produced singlet oxygen through photodynamic reactions and inhibited tumor growth in mice with minimal damage to normal cells [115].

Additionally, the glucose-derived CDs showed about 28% of doxorubicin loading and could be used for combined cancer therapy and diagnostics [69]. The ethanol modified bovine serum albumin-based CDs and doxorubicin delivery system exhibited a pH-dependent release phenomenon, and the released doxorubicin could be rapidly taken up by adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells [81]. The E. coli genomic DNA-based CDs could serve as a vehicle for delivering propidium iodide into S. cerevisiae and human embryonic kidney 293 cells [96].

There are some other applications of bio-based CDs having been investigated as well, such as preventing biofilm formation [101], promoting mung bean sprout growth [51] (Fig. 4f), improving algal astaxanthin production [79], enhancing oil recovery [136], and anti-fraudulent applications [65].

The Lactobacillus plantarum-based CDs were demonstrated to prevent E. coli biofilm but did not affect the growth of E. coli and animal lung AT-II cells, which could overcome the cytotoxicity issue in many current anti-biofilm agents [101]. Because of the CDs could significantly reduce the colonization and aggregation of E. coli on the surface, as well as show low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [101]. Sankaranarayanan et al. [51] found that the seed germination of mung beans became more vigorous with the addition of 25% brown macroalgal bio-oil-based CDs solutions, while a further increase in CDs concentration decreased the growth of mung beans. Moreover, Abu-Ghosh et al. [79] used bovine serum albumin-based CDs to enhance astaxanthin production during the culture of Haematococcus pluvialis. The astaxanthin production increased more than twofold to 66 mg/L from 29 mg/L of control by adding 1 mg/L bovine serum albumin-based CDs, even after a shorter cultivation time.

Additionally, the p-sulfonic acid calix[4]arene modified glucose-based CDs demonstrated excellent proficiency in providing high oil recovery of 17% at very low CDs concentrations of 0.01 wt% [136]. Because of the colloidal stability in aqueous and brine solutions, and low retention on the sand surface of CDs, they could alter rock wettability towards more water and create temporary log-jamming to let CDs recover oil trapped in tight reservoirs [136]. Additionally, the citric acid and ethanediamine modified poplar wood powder-based CDs solution was used as a stamp-pad and pen ink, which emitted a bright blue and stable light in the solid-state after drying when exposed to UV light, and proved to be suitable for anti-fraudulent applications [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Co-production of CDs and value-added products
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Tables 2 and 3, most hydrothermal CDs are prepared at temperatures between 140 °C and 240 °C, which mainly correspond to the reaction temperature range of the biomass hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) and hydrothermal liquefaction (HTL) processes [45,137]. In addition to obtaining CDs, the biomass hydrothermal process produces gas, organic liquid, and solid by-products as well, which are rarely reported in bio-based CDs preparation. While these products could be used as biofuels [137–139], such as biogas [140], bio-oil [141], and biochar [142].

During the hydrothermal process, part of the biomass could be converted into gases, mainly composed of CH4, H2, CO2, and CO [45]. Few studies are focusing on gaseous products during HTC and HTL processes because their gas proportion is quite low and mainly composed of CO2 and CO with low added value [142,143]. Bio-oil, the water-insoluble organic liquid product produced from the biomass hydrothermal process, which has the potential to replace heavy petroleum oil, be converted into high-quality distillate fuel [144,145], or be directly used as fuel [142,146]. Biochar is the carbon rich solid hydrothermal product with abundant surface functional groups, which can be used as solid fuel [147], soil conditioner [148], heavy metal fixer, supercapacitor electrode material, and anode material for lithium-ion batteries [142,143,149]. When using biochar as a solid fuel, it displays fewer negative effects of NOx and SOx pollution due to its lower nitrogen and sulfur content than many other fossil fuels [150]. Several studies have confirmed that the hydrothermal conditions (such as reaction temperature, reaction time, catalyst, biomass particle size, etc.) influenced yields and properties of the above multiphase products significantly [151–153]. Several studies have been done to produce multiphase hydrothermal products to use biomass more efficiently and economically. Such as microalgae and faecal sludge were co-hydrothermal liquefied to produce biocrude with yield up to 38% and higher heating value of 33.8 MJ/kg. This hydrothermal technology could recover 85.6% of energy and was confirmed to be an energy positivity system [154]. Furthermore, the microalgae were hydrothermally liquefied into 26.5 wt% bio-crude oil with a 2.4 energy consumption ratio, meanwhile, the byproduct hydrochar was used as anode material for lithium-ion batteries [155].

The life cycle assessment carried out by Bennion et al. [156] showed that the biomass hydrothermal process had a lower global warming potential impact when compared with other biomass conversion technology, such as pyrolysis [150,157], thereby making it a promising biomass utilization technology. However, although biomass hydrothermal technology related studies have lasted for around 100 years, its challenges, such as the high cost and the pollution of aqueous products, have limited the large-scale and practical application of this technology [158]. In response to this situation, the co-production of CDs and value-added biofuels will help progress the fascinating research field of biomass hydrothermal technology with the advantage of utilizing biomass hydrothermal multiphase products.

A small number of studies have tentatively confirmed the possibility of co-production of bio-based CDs and value-added products [8,159]. Guo et al. [159] prepared CDs and porous biochar through the microalgae hydrothermal process, which could be used in bioimaging and CO2 capture. In more detail, the CDs had low cytotoxicity and efficient cellular uptake, and displayed a strong two-photon fluorescence property. Furthermore, due to the large specific surface area (1396 m2/g) and high micropore volume (0.59 cm3/g) of the porous biochar, it demonstrated excellent CO2 capture capacity (4.2 mmol/g) at 25 °C (see Fig. 5a). In another study, Park et al. [8] co-produced CDs and nanoporous biochar via the empty fruit bunch hydrothermal process successfully. The CDs emitted bright green fluorescence and displayed a spherical shape of 1.6–3.0 nm size. Meanwhile, the nanoporous biochar showed a 1274.4 m2/g surface area and good supercapacitive performance (see Fig. 5b). Generally, it is assumed that the co-production of CDs and biofuels technology displays great development prospects and relevant research needs to be strengthened, although no systematic studies have been reported up till now.

The co-production provides new possibilities for using biomass comprehensively, thereby promoting the development of biomass energy utilization. However, several existing problems should be paid attention to for realizing the co-production with high application perspectives.

Firstly, the complex components (carbohydrate, protein, lignin, etc.) contained in biomass will undergo a series of chemical reactions during the hydrothermal process. Simultaneous with these reactions, the gas, liquid, and solid products are generated and consumed continuously. Especially, generation pathways of these multiphase products are still not clear, which makes it difficult to control the generation of CDs and biofuels through hydrothermal reaction conditions directly. Therefore, it is important to reveal the formation mechanism of multiphase products for regulating the generation of products during the hydrothermal process. Studies about biomass composition model compound hydrothermal conversion and theory simulations (such as molecular dynamics, reaction kinetics, and density functional theory) could help illustrate reaction pathways during the hydrothermal process. The hydrothermal conversion of biomass is mostly carried out in an autoclave, which is a “black box” process with high temperature and high pressure. Thus, conventional tests cannot fully reveal the regularity of biomass compositions' chemical reactions. The in-situ analysis provides a new way to analyze the formation process of CDs and biofuels by monitoring the hydrothermal process online. Therefore, it is very important to establish a visualized hydrothermal reactor [160,161], which could not only ensure the high temperature and high pressure conditions for hydrothermal reactions but also receive real-time monitoring signals for in-situ testing. For visual reactor, advanced instruments, such as high-resolution video microscope and infrared camera, can be used to monitor the changes in phase state and temperature distribution field during the hydrothermal process. In addition, the in-situ Raman test can be conducted to analyze the structural changes of chemical substances during hydrothermal reactions.

Secondly, most hydrothermal conversion processes are conducted in batch reactors and are difficult for heat recovery and large-scale application. While a continuous reactor with a heat recovery unit shows advantages of high energy recovery efficiency, high yields of CDs and biofuels, and low operating cost, which is suitable in industrial-scale applications [47,162]. The biomass slurry has complex rheological characteristics, viscosity, and thermal conductivity under hydrothermal conditions, which are different from the ideal fluid. In the hydrothermal reactor, the flow characteristics and heat transfer performances of biomass slurry significantly affect the temperature distributions in the reactor, thus affecting the hydrothermal reactions process and generating different hydrothermal products [163,164]. These variation products in turn affect the rheological properties and heat transfer performances of reactants along with the hydrothermal process. Therefore, in addition to the chemical reaction mechanism, not only the rheological and heat transfer properties of biomass slurry should be investigated, but also the relationships among fluid flow, heat transfer, and chemical reactions during the biomass hydrothermal process should be explored for the process intensification of the co-production of CDs and biofuels.

Thirdly, to enhance the utilization efficiency of biomass and to improve the economic value of hydrothermal technology, an integrated system for the co-production of CDs and biofuels should be constructed. In addition to experimental investigation, numerical simulation, such as computational fluid dynamics [165], is a useful approach for exploring interactions among hydrothermal reactions, and heat and mass transfer in biomass hydrothermal reactors. Additionally, machine learning provides new opportunities to biomass conversion technology, as it has the potential to accurately predict and optimize the highly complicated nonlinear CDs and biofuels co-production system in a short time [138]. Furthermore, comprehensive techno-economic and life cycle assessments [156,166] should be carried out to evaluate the economic viability and environmental impact of the co-production technology systematically and quantitatively.

Bio-based CDs as new-fashioned carbon materials have demonstrated huge application potential. The co-production of CDs and value-added biofuels via biomass hydrothermal conversion shows advantages to enhancing the economic feasibility and energy conversion efficiency of biomass hydrothermal technology. However, relevant studies focused on the co-production of CDs and biofuels are still not satisfactory and should be carried out. It is recommended to investigate the following research directions in the future.
•
The bio-based CDs were generated by decomposition of biomass, fragmentation and hydrolysis of carbohydrate/protein/lignin, cyclization, aromatic condensation, and polymerization reactions during the hydrothermal process. Further exploration focused on CDs formation mechanisms should be conducted to obtain bio-based CDs with high yield and excellent properties for applications. The in-situ test and higher resolution test should be emphasized to confirm useful reactions information (especially interactions among biomass components) during the hydrothermal process.
•
The bio-based CDs displayed 0.7–50.0 nm particle size, contained several functional groups, were doped with multiple elements, showed low toxicity and high biocompatibility, and exhibited special optical performance for applying in biological imaging, sensor, photoelectric device, catalyst, disease treatment, etc. There are still many unknown relationships among the compositions of biomass, structures and properties of bio-based CDs, and application abilities of bio-based CDs, which need to be further explored.
•
The co-production of bio-based CDs and biofuels showed the advantages of obtaining CDs more economically by full utilization of biomass. To realize the co-production assumption, research about multiphase products formation mechanisms, the heat and mass transfer process in hydrothermal reactors, in-situ monitoring of the hydrothermal process, and continuous hydrothermal systems should be investigated. Additionally, an integrated system for the co-production of CDs and biofuels should be established.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jingmiao Zhang obtained her Ph.D. degree from the Graduate School of Agricultural and Life Sciences, The University of Tokyo in 2020. She is currently an assistant professor in the School of Energy and Power Engineering, Chongqing University. Her research interests are focused on the preparation of value-added bio-based materials through biomass thermochemical conversion and wastewater treatment by biomass technology.

Ao Xia is a professor in the School of Energy and Power Engineering at Chongqing University. He serves as an associate editor for Frontiers in Bioengineering and Biotechnology and Frontiers in Energy Research, and also serves as an editorial board member for Energies and Coal Science and Technology. He completed his Ph.D. research in 2013 in Energy Engineering from Zhejiang University. He worked as a post-doctoral researcher in the Environmental Research Institute at University College Cork during 2014-2016. His research interests include biofuels, fermentation, hydrothermal process, carbon dioxide utilization, and biocatalysis.

Xianqing Zhu received his Ph.D. degree from the School of Energy and Power Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology in June 2018. He is currently an associate professor in the School of Energy and Power Engineering, Chongqing University. His major research topics focused on (1) degradative solvent extraction and upgrading of biomass waste, (2) solid waste pyrolysis, gasification technologies, and CFD simulation, (3) thermochemical recycling of spent lithium-ion batteries.

Yun Huang obtained her Ph.D. degree in engineering from Zhejiang University in 2014. In the same year, she joined the School of Energy and Power Engineering, Chongqing University. As an associate professor, she has undertaken 2 research projects supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China and 1 research project supported by the State's Key Project of Research and Development Plan. Her research mainly focuses on key issues about thermal physics in energy and environmental technology, CO2 fixation and wastewater treatment by microalgae, flow and mass transport in microalgae suspension and biofilm, microalgae application in wastewater treatment.

Xun Zhu is a professor in the School of Energy and Power Engineering at Chongqing University. She is a heat and mass transfer professional committee member of the CSET, a guest editor of three international journals, and a reviewer of multiple international journals. Prof. Zhu mainly engages in fuel cell technology, micro-scale multi-phase flow, heat and mass transfer, microbial energy conversion and energy saving, and other areas of research. Prof. Zhu had won second prize of The Natural Science of Chongqing, second prize of The National Energy Science and Technology Progress, third prize of Science and Technology Progress of China Power Investment Corporation.

Qiang Liao is a professor in the School of Energy and Power Engineering at Chongqing University. He is also the Director of the Institute of Engineering Thermophysics and Dean of the School of Energy and Power Engineering at Chongqing University. His research focuses on bioenergy, bioreactors, and heat and mass transfer. He serves as the subject editor of Energy, guest editor for International Journal of Hydrogen Energy and International Journal of Green Energy, and is on the editorial board of twelve journals such as Applied Thermal Engineering. He has served as temporal Representative of China in International Energy Agency (IEA) Hydrogen Implementing Agreement (HIA) Task 34. Prof. Liao has taken charge of 11 Natural Science Foundations of China, 2 National Key Projects, and 11 Provincial Key Projects.
